one_line_takeaway: SGBA combines a benign 'scapegoat' decoy trigger plus a split two-part real trigger with training-time weight-variance clipping to simultaneously defeat both trigger-reverse-engineering and ML-based backdoor detectors, cutting average detection from >90% to <10% while keeping near-BadNets attack success.
key_finding: SGBA evades all five state-of-the-art model-inspection schemes while retaining strong attack effectiveness. Average detection rates drop from >90% (BadNets) to <10% (SGBA). Against Neural Cleanse, SGBA's Anomaly Index is at or below benign models and its detection rate is 0% (MNIST, CIFAR10) / 10% (GTSRB) versus 100% for BadNets. Against ABS, SGBA detection rate is 0% / 0% / 10% versus BadNets 100% / 60% / 70%. Against TABOR on GTSRB, detection rate is 0% (average Anomaly Index 1.197, threshold 2). Against the ML-based MNTD-Robust, SGBA detection rate is only 9% (MNIST) and 3% (CIFAR10), essentially equal to the false-positive rate. Attack performance stays high: MNIST ASR 99.69% (clean acc 98.94%), GTSRB ASR 93.68% (clean acc 97.00%), and on ImageNet ASR 99.37% (clean acc 92.41%) with REASR 0.0581 close to the benign 0.0316 and far below BadNets 0.2516.
core_contribution: The paper proposes SGBA (Stealthy Scapegoat Backdoor Attack), a patch-based trojan attack on outsourced DNNs designed to simultaneously evade both families of pre-deployment model-inspection defenses while preserving the original semantics of patch-based trojans (arbitrary trigger pattern, universal effective domain). Its own contribution has two indispensable components. (1) A 'scapegoat' trigger to defeat trigger-reverse-engineering defenses: the attacker first trains a clean model, reverse-engineers the benign per-class trigger, and uses the benign trigger of the target class as a decoy that these defenses reverse instead of the real trigger; benign triggers of non-target classes are also injected so all reversed triggers look normal. The real trigger is split into two separated, interdependent sub-patterns (backdoor fires only when both are present) positioned so the scapegoat's area cannot cover both parts at once, defeating even clipping-based improvements of Neural Cleanse. (2) A weight-variance limitation applied during training to defeat ML-based (meta-classifier) defenses: the attacker clips each layer's weight variance below a threshold w x (mean benign-model variance) so the trojan model's weight distribution stays within the variance envelope of clean models, removing the signal such classifiers rely on. The authors are careful to distinguish their approach from prior evasive attacks (composition/TaCT/reflection/bit-flip/image-scaling/WaNet/DFST) in the Related Work section, arguing those sacrifice trigger universality, require special hardware, or are hard to deploy physically.
threat_model: Outsourced-training / model-supply-chain setting. Adversary (the model trainer / third-party marketplace provider) has full control of the training workflow and can access and modify the training set, producing a poisoned model F where F(x+tau)=target class Ct for any input carrying trigger tau while F(x)=true label otherwise. Defender receives only the trained model: full white-box access to all parameters and weights plus a small clean holdout dataset (below 10% of the clean training set) for testing, but no ability to observe or interfere with the training process. The adversarial goal is a stealthy patch-based backdoor that (a) behaves like a BadNets patch backdoor with a small, arbitrary, universal trigger and (b) escapes both trigger-reverse-engineering schemes (Neural Cleanse and improvements) and ML-based schemes (MNTD).
limitations: On CIFAR10 the attack must trade off effectiveness for stealth against MNTD: ASR falls to 85.69% and clean accuracy to 75.28% (from BadNets' 97.55% / 83.52%) to reach a ~97% escape rate, showing weight limitation is not free. The limitation coefficient w must be carefully hand-tuned (over-limitation harms the scapegoat scheme, too-loose fails to evade). Defense evaluation is incomplete by dataset (TABOR only on GTSRB owing to hyperparameter tuning cost; MNTD only on MNIST/CIFAR10 because GTSRB would take >150 days), so cross-dataset generalization against those specific defenses is not fully demonstrated. Evaluation is confined to image classification. The attack presumes a strong adversary with full training-workflow control. The authors themselves note SGBA is beatable in principle by improving the reverse-engineering search strategy, and mixing SGBA models into MNTD's shadow set can also degrade detection of other attacks (indicating the defense-side effect is dataset/setup sensitive rather than universally robust). Evaluation is against 2019-2020-era defenses.

datasets_used: ImageNet subset (50 categories x 400 images each, ResNet50)
quantitative_results: MNIST: SGBA attack success rate 99.69%, clean classification accuracy 98.94% (benign 98.80%, BadNets ASR 100.00%/acc 99.03%)
CIFAR10: SGBA ASR 85.69%, clean acc 75.28% (down from BadNets 97.55%/83.52%) traded for ~97% MNTD escape rate
GTSRB: SGBA ASR 93.68%, clean acc 97.00% (benign 95.15%, BadNets ASR 90.70%)
ImageNet: SGBA ASR 99.37%, clean acc 92.41%; REASR 0.0581 vs benign 0.0316 and BadNets 0.2516
Neural Cleanse detection rate: SGBA 0%/0%/10% (MNIST/CIFAR10/GTSRB) vs BadNets 100% on all
ABS detection rate: SGBA 0%/0%/10% vs BadNets 100%/60%/70%; SGBA REASR 0.3584/0.3770/0.4374 near benign
TABOR on GTSRB: detection rate 0%, average Anomaly Index 1.197 (threshold 2); ~8 hours per model
MNTD-Robust detection rate: SGBA 9% (MNIST, = 9% FPR) and 3% (CIFAR10, = 3% FPR) vs trojM 94%/92% and trojB 97%/42%
Overall average detection rate falls from >90% (BadNets) to <10% (SGBA)
Weight-variance study contrasts 2304 benign vs 2304 malicious models on MNIST; MNTD used 4608 total shadow models per dataset (256+256 as validation)
baselines_compared: BadNets (Gu et al.) as the basic patch-based trojan attack baseline
Benign (clean) models as the non-backdoored reference
trojM (modification backdoor) and trojB (blending backdoor) as additional MNTD comparison attacks
Defenses evaded: Neural Cleanse, improved NC with clipped search (cutting and hollow), TABOR, ABS, MNTD-Robust (One-Pixel Signature also argued to fail)
